title: Creating a Company Letterhead
description: Design a professional company letterhead that can be reused for all business correspondence, with watermarks and section breaks.
objectives: Design a clean header with company details
Use colors and fonts that reflect brand identity
Add watermarks and section breaks for different page layouts
content:
What Is a Letterhead?

A letterhead is the top section of a business document that displays the company name, logo, address, phone number, email, and website. It appears on every official letter, memo, and invoice. A well-designed letterhead builds trust and makes the company look established.

Design Principles

  • Keep it clean: Do not overcrowd the header. White space is your friend.
  • Use one or two colors: A professional blue, green, or black is enough. Avoid bright colors.
  • Make the company name prominent: It should be the largest or boldest text in the header.
  • Include essential contact info: Address, phone, and email are standard.

Building It in Word

Use the Header area (Insert > Header) so the letterhead appears automatically on every page. Insert a table if you want to align the logo and text side by side. Add a subtle bottom border to separate the header from the body text. Save the file as a Word Template (.dotx) so you can reuse it for every new letter.

Watermarks and Section Breaks

A watermark is a faint image or text that appears behind the document content, often used for 'CONFIDENTIAL' or 'DRAFT' markings. Go to Design > Watermark to choose a preset or create a custom one. Section breaks let you have different headers, footers, or page layouts on different pages. For example, the first page might have a full letterhead while following pages have a simpler header. Go to Layout > Breaks > Next Page Section Break to start a new section.

Workplace Scenario

You need to create a letterhead for 'GreenLeaf Consulting.' You design the header with the company name in bold, a teal accent color, and contact details below. You add a 'DRAFT' watermark on early versions. You use a section break so that only the first page has the full letterhead, and page 2 onwards has a simpler header with just the company name. You save this as a template (.dotx) so every new letter starts with the correct formatting.
exercise: Design a letterhead for a fictional company called 'GreenLeaf Consulting' in Word. Include the company name, address (123 Main Street, Cape Town), phone number, email, and a logo placeholder. Use a teal accent color. Add a 'CONFIDENTIAL' watermark. Use a section break so only the first page has the full letterhead. Save as a Word Template (.dotx).

quiz.question: Where should you place the letterhead so it appears on every page?
quiz.explanation: The header area repeats automatically on every page of the document.

quiz.question: What is the best file format to save a letterhead for reuse?
quiz.explanation: A Word Template (.dotx) lets you create new documents with the letterhead already in place.

quiz.question: Which color choice is most appropriate for a professional letterhead?
quiz.explanation: Conservative, professional colors like black, blue, or dark green convey trust and professionalism.

quiz.question: What is the purpose of a section break in a letter?
options: To apply different headers or layouts on different pages
quiz.explanation: Section breaks allow different headers, footers, and page layouts within the same document.
